question: The Nile is the longest river in the world.
explanation: The Nile runs approximately 6,650 km through northeastern Africa and is the traditionally recognised longest river. Its exact length compared to the Amazon is still debated by geographers using different measurement methods.

question: The Amazon River discharges more water into the sea than any other river on Earth.
explanation: Despite not being the longest river, the Amazon discharges around 209,000 cubic metres of water per second — roughly 20% of all fresh water that flows into the world's oceans, more than the next seven largest rivers combined.

question: Russia is the largest country in the world by land area.
explanation: Russia spans approximately 17.1 million km², covering more than one-eighth of Earth's inhabited land area and stretching across eleven time zones from Eastern Europe to the Pacific.

question: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's land combined.
explanation: The Pacific Ocean covers about 165 million km², which exceeds the combined land area of all Earth's continents (roughly 150 million km²). It is by far the largest geographic feature on the planet.

question: Africa is the largest continent by land area.
explanation: Asia is Earth's largest continent at about 44.6 million km², followed by Africa at 30.3 million km². Africa is the second-largest continent by area and holds the largest population of any continent.

question: The blue whale is the largest animal known to have ever existed on Earth.
explanation: Blue whales can reach up to 30 metres in length and weigh as much as 200 tonnes, making them the largest animals known to have ever existed — larger even than the biggest dinosaurs on record.

question: The cheetah is the fastest land animal.
explanation: Cheetahs can reach speeds of up to 120 km/h in short sprinting bursts and can accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h in approximately three seconds, making them the fastest land animal by a wide margin.

question: The Great Wall of China is clearly visible from space with the naked eye.
explanation: Contrary to popular belief, the Great Wall is too narrow (typically 4–5 metres wide) to be resolved by the naked eye from low Earth orbit. Multiple astronauts, including Chinese astronaut Yang Liwei, have confirmed they could not see it.

question: Tokyo is the most populous metropolitan area in the world.
explanation: The Greater Tokyo Area is home to roughly 37–38 million people, making it consistently the largest metropolitan area in the world by population — ahead of Delhi, Shanghai, and São Paulo.

question: The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert on Earth.
explanation: The Sahara covers approximately 9.2 million km² across North Africa, making it the world's largest hot desert. Antarctica is technically a larger desert overall (cold deserts count), but the Sahara is the largest by the conventional 'hot desert' definition.

question: Gold is the most abundant metal found in the Earth's crust.
explanation: Aluminium is the most abundant metal in the Earth's crust, comprising about 8% of its mass. Gold is actually extremely rare, occurring at only a few parts per billion — which is a major reason it is so valuable.

question: Elephants are the largest living land animals on Earth.
explanation: African elephants are the largest living land animals, with adult males reaching up to 4 metres at the shoulder and weighing up to 7,000 kg. Asian elephants are slightly smaller but still the second-largest land animal.

question: Antarctica is classified as a desert.
explanation: A desert is defined by low precipitation, not temperature. Antarctica receives less than 200 mm of precipitation per year, qualifying it as the world's largest desert at about 14.2 million km² — larger than the Sahara.

question: Mandarin Chinese is the most spoken language in the world by number of native speakers.
explanation: Mandarin Chinese has approximately 920 million native speakers, making it the most widely spoken first language in the world — well ahead of Spanish (around 480 million) and English (around 380 million native speakers).

question: The country of Nigeria has a larger population than Russia.
explanation: Nigeria has a population of over 220 million people, while Russia has approximately 145 million. Despite Russia's vast land area — the largest in the world — Nigeria's population is nearly 50% larger.

question: Global child mortality (under age 5) has more than halved since 1990.
explanation: The global under-5 mortality rate fell from 93 deaths per 1,000 live births in 1990 to around 38 by 2019 — a decline of more than 50%, representing millions of children's lives saved annually through vaccines, nutrition, and healthcare access.

question: Global average life expectancy at birth is now over 70 years.
explanation: Global average life expectancy reached approximately 73 years by 2019, up from around 46 years in 1950. Even accounting for setbacks from the COVID-19 pandemic, it remains above 70 years globally.

question: The majority of global deaths are now caused by non-communicable diseases.
explanation: Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) such as cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, and chronic respiratory disease now account for around 74% of all deaths globally, according to WHO data — a significant shift from infectious diseases a century ago.

question: Vaccines prevent an estimated 2 to 3 million deaths per year globally.
explanation: WHO estimates that vaccines currently prevent 2–3 million deaths each year from diseases such as diphtheria, tetanus, whooping cough, influenza, and measles — and could prevent a further 1.5 million deaths if global coverage improved.

question: The global extreme poverty rate has declined significantly since 1990.
explanation: The share of the world's population living on less than $2.15 per day (2017 PPP) fell from roughly 38% in 1990 to under 9% by 2019. Progress has slowed since the COVID-19 pandemic, but the long-run trend is a dramatic reduction in extreme poverty.

question: Tuberculosis remains one of the top 10 causes of death worldwide.
explanation: Tuberculosis consistently ranks among the top 10 global killers. In 2022, an estimated 1.3 million people died from TB, making it one of the deadliest infectious diseases globally alongside HIV/AIDS — despite being largely preventable and treatable.

question: Global literacy rates have increased substantially since 1970.
explanation: Global adult literacy has risen substantially, from around 56% in 1970 to approximately 87% by 2022. Gains have been especially large in South and East Asia, driven by expanded primary education access and economic development.

question: Malaria deaths have more than doubled since 2000.
explanation: Malaria deaths have actually declined significantly since 2000, largely due to the scale-up of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, and improved antimalarial treatments. Annual deaths fell from around 900,000 in 2000 to roughly 620,000 by 2020.

question: Global maternal mortality has decreased significantly since 1990.
explanation: Global maternal mortality fell from an estimated 339 deaths per 100,000 live births in 2000 to around 223 by 2020 — a reduction of approximately 34%. Progress has been uneven, with Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia accounting for the majority of remaining deaths.

question: More people globally now have access to mobile phones than to safely managed sanitation.
explanation: As of the early 2020s, roughly 5.3 billion people had mobile phones, while only about 3.5 billion had access to safely managed sanitation facilities — a striking disparity that reflects the relative ease and economics of mobile infrastructure versus public health investment.

question: Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death globally.
explanation: Cardiovascular diseases including heart disease and stroke are responsible for roughly 17.9 million deaths per year — about 32% of all global deaths. They have been the leading cause of death globally for several decades.

question: The majority of people who lack access to electricity live in Sub-Saharan Africa.
explanation: Approximately 600 million people lack access to electricity globally, and roughly 80% of them live in Sub-Saharan Africa. Rural communities in the region are disproportionately affected, though electrification rates have been improving steadily.

question: Diabetes prevalence worldwide has roughly doubled since 1980.
explanation: The global prevalence of diabetes rose from 4.7% in 1980 to around 9.3% in 2019. The rise is driven largely by increased obesity, sedentary lifestyles, and population ageing — and the condition now affects over 400 million adults worldwide.

question: The global obesity rate is higher today than the global hunger rate.
explanation: Worldwide, over 1 billion adults are now classified as obese, and obesity-related conditions cause an estimated 4–5 million deaths per year. In contrast, roughly 700,000–800,000 deaths are directly attributable to undernutrition, making obesity a larger mortality burden in aggregate.

question: HIV/AIDS killed more people in 2022 than it did in 2002.
explanation: HIV/AIDS deaths peaked globally around 2004–2006 at roughly 2 million per year and have since declined to around 630,000 by 2022, largely due to the scale-up of antiretroviral therapy — one of global public health's major successes.

question: Pigs are widely regarded by researchers as more cognitively complex than dogs.
explanation: Research suggests pigs outperform dogs on a range of cognitive tasks including learning symbolic languages, using mirrors, and solving complex problems. Their intelligence is often compared to that of a young human child, and they are considered among the most cognitively sophisticated land animals.

question: Octopuses have three hearts.
explanation: Octopuses have three hearts: two branchial hearts pump blood through each of the two gills, while the systemic heart pumps oxygenated blood to the rest of the body. Interestingly, two of the three hearts stop beating when the octopus swims, which is why octopuses prefer crawling.

question: Crows can recognize and remember individual human faces.
explanation: Studies by Dr. John Marzluff at the University of Washington found that crows memorise the faces of people who have captured or threatened them and will scold those individuals for years afterwards — even teaching other crows to recognise and mob the same faces.

question: Elephants have been documented engaging in behaviors consistent with mourning their dead.
explanation: Researchers have documented elephants returning to the remains of deceased family members, touching the bones with their trunks, and standing silently nearby for extended periods — behaviours widely interpreted as mourning-like responses, documented consistently across multiple wild populations.

question: The majority of land animals raised for food globally live on industrial factory farms.
explanation: Various estimates suggest that 70–80% of farmed land animals globally are raised in intensive industrial systems. The exact figure varies by country and species, but factory farming dominates global animal agriculture by sheer volume of animals.

question: Dolphins have been observed using tools in the wild.
explanation: Bottlenose dolphins in Shark Bay, Australia, carry marine sponges on their snouts to protect themselves while foraging on the seafloor — one of the clearest documented cases of tool use in non-primate wild animals, a behaviour that appears to be culturally transmitted within certain matrilines.

question: Laboratory rats have demonstrated empathy-like behavior by helping other rats in distress.
explanation: A 2011 study by Bartal et al. in Science found that rats would work to free a trapped cagemate even with no direct reward, and would then share chocolate with the freed rat rather than eating it all themselves — a finding widely cited as evidence of empathy-like behaviour in rodents.

question: There are more chickens on Earth at any given time than humans.
explanation: There are approximately 33 billion chickens alive on Earth at any given time, compared to roughly 8 billion humans. Chickens are thought to be the most numerous bird species on the planet, a direct result of industrial-scale poultry farming.

question: Fish have nociceptors and display behavioral responses consistent with feeling pain.
explanation: A 2016 review in Animal Cognition concluded that fish have the neurological apparatus to detect and respond to painful stimuli, including nociceptors and avoidance behaviours. The question of whether they subjectively experience pain remains philosophically contested, but the behavioural and physiological evidence is substantial.

question: The majority of global antibiotic use by volume is in livestock, not humans.
explanation: The WHO estimates that approximately 73% of all antimicrobials sold globally are used in animals raised for food, primarily to promote growth and prevent disease in crowded conditions. This overuse is a major driver of antimicrobial resistance in both animal and human pathogens.

question: Chimpanzees have demonstrated the ability to learn and use human sign language.
explanation: Washoe, a chimpanzee studied by Beatrix and Allen Gardner in the 1960s, learned hundreds of American Sign Language signs and used them spontaneously to communicate. Later research extended similar findings to other great apes, including gorillas and orangutans.

question: Honeybees have been shown to exhibit pessimistic cognitive biases after stressful events.
explanation: A 2011 study in Current Biology found that honeybees subjected to a simulated predator attack subsequently displayed pessimistic judgement biases in ambiguous situations — a finding widely interpreted as evidence that insects can experience something analogous to negative emotional states.

question: Most farmed salmon are raised in open-net ocean pens rather than fully enclosed tanks.
explanation: The vast majority of the world's farmed Atlantic salmon — roughly 2.5 million tonnes per year — is raised in open-net sea cages anchored in coastal waters, allowing waste, parasites such as sea lice, and escaped fish to interact freely with the surrounding marine environment.

question: Insects make up more than half of all known animal species on Earth.
explanation: Insects account for approximately 1 million of the roughly 1.5–2 million named animal species — around 60–70% of all known animal species. Beetles alone make up nearly 25% of all described animal species, prompting geneticist J.B.S. Haldane's famous quip about God's 'inordinate fondness for beetles.'

question: Parrots are the only non-human animals known to produce entirely novel sentences.
explanation: Multiple species — including great apes trained in sign language, some cetaceans, and certain parrots — have shown abilities to combine symbols or sounds in novel ways. No single species holds an exclusive claim to this capacity, and the evidence across species is ongoing and debated.

question: The term 'artificial intelligence' was coined in the 1950s.
explanation: The term 'artificial intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy at the Dartmouth Conference in 1956, where he organised the first dedicated workshop on the field. McCarthy is widely considered one of the founding fathers of AI.

question: AlphaGo defeated a professional world champion at the game of Go before 2020.
explanation: DeepMind's AlphaGo defeated world champion Lee Sedol 4–1 in March 2016 — a watershed moment for deep reinforcement learning and widely seen as arriving a decade earlier than most experts had predicted.

question: GPT-4 was publicly released in 2023.
explanation: OpenAI released GPT-4 on 14 March 2023. It demonstrated significantly improved reasoning, coding, and instruction-following capabilities compared to GPT-3.5, and became the engine behind the upgraded version of ChatGPT.

question: DeepMind's AlphaFold achieved a major breakthrough in protein structure prediction in 2020.
explanation: DeepMind's AlphaFold 2 was unveiled at CASP14 in November 2020 and published in Nature in 2021. It predicted the 3D structures of virtually all known proteins with high accuracy, solving a 50-year-old grand challenge in structural biology.

question: The Transformer architecture, which underlies modern large language models, was introduced before 2015.
explanation: The Transformer architecture was introduced in the landmark 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need' by Vaswani et al. at Google — not before 2015. It replaced recurrent networks for most NLP tasks and became the foundation for BERT, GPT, and all subsequent large language models.

question: AI systems have been used to discover novel antibiotic candidates.
explanation: In 2020, MIT researchers used a deep learning model to identify halicin, a potent antibiotic effective against drug-resistant bacteria, screening over 100 million candidate molecules in a matter of days — a task that would have taken decades with traditional methods.

question: An AI system defeated world-champion human players at poker before 2020.
explanation: Libratus, developed at Carnegie Mellon University, defeated four top professional poker players at heads-up no-limit Texas Hold'em in January 2017 — demonstrating AI's ability to handle imperfect-information games with bluffing, a significant step beyond chess or Go.

question: Fully autonomous self-driving cars at SAE Level 5 are commercially available in most major cities.
explanation: As of 2025, no manufacturer offers fully autonomous (SAE Level 5) commercial vehicles. Waymo and a few others operate limited Level 4 robotaxi services in specific US cities under controlled conditions, but full autonomy in all environments and weather conditions remains unrealised.

question: The first AI program capable of playing checkers was created in the 1950s.
explanation: Arthur Samuel at IBM developed the first checkers-playing program in 1952. By 1959 it could learn from experience and beat strong amateur players — pioneering the concept of machine learning decades before the term became widely used.

question: China now produces more AI research publications per year than the United States.
explanation: China has outpaced the US in AI research publication volume since around 2018, according to analyses by groups such as the Allen Institute for AI. However, citation-weighted impact metrics show a more nuanced picture, with US institutions still producing more highly cited work.

question: More than half of all internet traffic is estimated to come from automated bots.
explanation: Imperva's 2022 Bad Bot Report estimated that 47.4% of all internet traffic was generated by bots, with malicious bots accounting for 26.6% of total traffic. Similar estimates from other firms consistently place bot traffic above 40–50% of total web activity.

question: Natural language processing became a recognized AI research area before the year 2000.
explanation: Natural language processing traces its roots to the 1950s, with Alan Turing's 1950 paper proposing the imitation game and the Georgetown–IBM experiment in 1954 automatically translating Russian sentences into English. NLP has been a core AI research area for over 70 years.

question: IBM's Deep Blue defeated world chess champion Garry Kasparov in 1997.
explanation: Deep Blue defeated Kasparov over a six-game match in May 1997 — the first time a reigning world champion had been defeated by a computer under standard tournament conditions. The match was a cultural milestone and drew enormous global media coverage.

question: Generative AI tools such as image generators were widely available to the public before 2020.
explanation: DALL-E launched in January 2021, Midjourney in July 2022, and Stable Diffusion in August 2022 — all after 2020. While GAN-based research existed earlier, accessible consumer-facing generative image tools did not exist before 2021.

question: Large language models are primarily trained using reinforcement learning from scratch.
explanation: Large language models are primarily trained using self-supervised learning on large text corpora (pretraining), often followed by supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). Reinforcement learning plays a secondary role and is not the primary training paradigm.
